speaker: interviewer
text: Speaking with Dora Nesselroth.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war was over and you were liberated, and you found your brother as you told us--

speaker: interviewee
text: I found him and I took him home.

speaker: interviewer
text: You took him home, and who--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was on the-- the-- on the-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the-- the roof-- on the roof on the-- with the other boys. And the Russians used to come over and throw everything away together with the passengers sometimes, what they brought with them. Because I bought for my brother a blanket, a cushion, a pillow. I brought a pair of slacks for him. I bought pajamas. I bought utensils to eat with, and something else-- little things. And I put everything in a-- in a valise. And I have to take him out from there. So he couldn't walk. To go to the railroad station, is what-- about 3 miles to go there. So I took the luggage first. I put it-- I go about 10-- 10 feet away from him. And I go back and I took him on my back. And I put the weight again and again. It took me about-- I don't know how long. And the other boy used to walk. He used to walk. So after that, we-- so I told you about that from Prague. From Prague, we-- we went there. The Russian came over, a young man came over. And I said, listen, I have-- do me a favor. Take care of us. Because you see, these two boys, they're very sick. And besides that I have a lot-- I trust you because you look exactly like Stalin, because they loved Stalin [INAUDIBLE]. And he said, don't worry about it. I will not let anybody do for you-- interfere with you driving home. And so he really helped us, until the-- the border, [NON-ENGLISH], now I remember, at [NON-ENGLISH], From there and the next day, in the morning we got the-- the-- the train was empty. But later on, a lot of people came. We had sitting, a very good place where room to sit there, enough room. And we came to Czestochowa, back to Czestochowa. We moved back because I had a- a work. I work with the Polish government agency, POP, this is Polish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] agency-- Polish-- [POLISH] press agency. And I worked there, the only woman. I was a sport correspondent. And so this was very good for me. Because it was like to go a psychiatrist, because I was always out, always think that my father is not-- is still alive, and he will come back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was-- who was with you then at that point with your family? I mean who in your family was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my sister was already with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- and who else?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And anyone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my cousin, the one cousin who survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was you, your sister, your brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Cousin and two-- and two women from-- from Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother with them too.

speaker: interviewer
text: With you also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: After-- after the war was all over, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what-- where did you live? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved in from-- from Czestochowa to Katowice where I had a job. And then I worked there. I worked there about a year and a half. And then they asked me to join the Communist Party. And I said, no, I'm going now to the university. I cannot join. I'm going to the university. And I went back to Lodz, and I went to a dental school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in-- in Katowice. He was working. He was-- of two provinces he was Secretary of the Zionist organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got married in 1948, in Jelenia Gora. So my mother went to Jelenia Gora and I was in Lodz, and then I came to Jelenia Gora. We open a factory there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the United States, we came in 1962, in January 14.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We settle in New York. And I went, and I had my license in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: License for what?

speaker: interviewee
text: For being an x-ray technician.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. How did you happen to come to Alabama?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband has-- he transferred. They transferred him from-- from New York to-- to Leeds, Alabama.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say they transferred him. Whom to you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: They work in the-- there the factory in the laboratory.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see--

speaker: interviewee
text: And also for-- this is an industry where they need to plate, plate things, and polishing, and different things they did there. And my husband was-- had-- he had to build a new department in the beginning. And he built up a new department with 50 beds, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beds, because solutions-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, for plating-- Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: For plating for different kind of work to do, for cleaning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when was that that you moved to Alabama?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In 1965. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- what you think of Alabama when you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you how did you feel about when you moved here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- you know, I left my sister, and my brother. It was very hard in the beginning. But I rather think with the children I was very happy. Because everything, what happened in New York, was too fast. And here it was slowly. They always came about two years later, two years later, three years later. It was with everything with the-- drugs, with everything, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it coming to Alabama at that time as a survivor? Did you-- was that difficult for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was difficult. Because it was difficult because I promised myself I'm not going to move anymore, because the movement was from one-- you know, from one camp to the other was a big deal. This reminds you when you hear the wheels in the train going and going, and you feel like-- God help me-- like you go someplace that you're not supposed to go there. With the-- with the airplane, I didn't have that much. But listen, when I work in New York in a private office by three doctors, Jewish doctors, and Fleischer, London-- he died later of cancer. And the third one I forget the name. But they were fantastic to me. And they said let your husband go. If you don't like it there, come back and you have the job back. And so my husband went, came over here in April. And I wait until the-- the children will finish school. And I work with Dr. Fleischer. And he gave me another job with another-- because I worked there four hours a day. And they gave me another job for another four hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I have time to accumulate money to buy me a table, a kitchen table I needed for this room there. And me, when we-- we bought this house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We got lost to-- to look for houses. We didn't ask anybody to help us. It was terrible. I only asked the distance between the school and the synagogue. So the school and the synagogue was in the middle. But here I have the school very close, synagogue was on the other side. So in the beginning the people were very, very nice; very nice, all of them. And so here the neighborhood was fantastic too. And this is a question they asked me, how come you moved over here. I said, did-- did you see working class people? They said, yes. So, what? Working class people cannot make millions of dollars. They cannot make millions of dollars. So they're exactly like you. So I'm exactly like you. I work very hard. I work 12 hours a day. So here I work. When I received the citizen of the United States, a lady who gave us a little flag, what else they gave me? And a book, some kind of a book with it. And she remembered my name and my profession. And she said, then I received a telephone call from the office of Dr. [? Egan ?] that they need an x-ray technician if I can come and work there. So I went, and I got my job from New York to here. Here I had to have another license. It was not enough to have license in New York, but you have here have another license. You mentioned your children. How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, Rosalie and Ruth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are they now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosalie is with-- this is the older daughter. She is-- lived in Atlanta, Georgia. And she has two children, Anna and Jackie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your other daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other, Ruth has three children. She married a doctor. And-- and my younger daughter met an engineer from Georgia Tech, a very good engineer. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your older daughter now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Atlanta, Georgia.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean your younger daughter, where is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is in Jerusalem. She's working also like a counselor to the newcomers from different kind of-- from different kind of countries, from Europe, from Argentina, from Brazil, from Asia, from everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there--

speaker: interviewee
text: They came into new immigrants.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Would you like to say anything? Is there anything you'd like to add that-- that we haven't covered, or that you haven't had a chance to talk about, just at the end of this interview now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is the end of the interview. I would like to say that after the war, people went through-- they finded out the terrible thing what happened, and they still didn't believe what we saw-- what we-- what we did. But I have to-- the only thing to tell about it, I would like to see people who would understand the horrible thing what the Nazis did to us, they killed 6 million Jews and-- and 5 million others for only-- the others, they didn't believe in-- in Nazism.

speaker: interviewee
text: And us, they killed because we were Jewish people. And the children, why they forget already? We had to do something for that reason. It is a constant reminder, they came out from this terrible thing. They-- they teach the children that Hitler was not a-- a murderer, that he was a good man, that the Holocaust doesn't exist. That's not true. We are witnesses. We were there. We are the one survivor who buried in our mind, the whole-- the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: We know the first hand that it was a Holocaust. It was a terrible Holocaust. It still is in our mind. The Holocaust still exists in my mind. And I beg that we can fight those people who-- who parade here in the United States, in the whole world, with swastikas on their-- on their arms, and with the hoods on their heads, the white hoods on their heads. And they are the one who would kill for nothing, with hatred and everything. We had to talk to our children for them to fight for our humanitarian reason that people who hate are very dangerous. We don't want to kill. We want to teach. And I think that everyone who goes and talks to those people has to give a message. And this message has to be that not to forget the past. If you forget the past, you don't have a future. This is mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, my grandfather. This was in 1920. And this is his whole family who were together because one of his daughters went-- this is his daughter-- went to America with six children, so the whole family. No. This is not his daughter. This is not the daughter. The daughter is on the other side here. She-- went to-- but her-- she-- and the rest are those aunts. We have aunts, I don't know her name. I forgot. This is the other-- this is a wife of my uncle's. And this is Rozja, I think. This is Ruchcia. This is her husband, Zejlik. My grandfather, I never know him. Because he died later. This is aunt, I don't remember her name. This is her daughter. This is her daughter-in-law. This is my father and my mother. This is my sister. And my father was-- look at him. He's a young man. My mother, and my sister was sick. In 1930 she died of typhus. It was an epidemic at that time, and she died. [SIGHS] This was taken, the picture was taken outside my grandfather's home. That's on the right side.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I-- my cousin who lived in Israel before the war, he came to-- to Palestine. He had an original picture of that, because his mother is here too. And he sent me a copy from his-- from the picture, the original he had it. And he died two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- this picture was taken in 1938, in high school. This is a-- from high school an ID card photo. I was 16 years old. They came by photographer at school. And I found it also this in-- in Canada I think, or in Israel, my little picture. I gave it to her, to this lady who, who paint these things. And she-- she lost it. And I didn't have it. In 1976, my husband-- I don't give-- my husband give. This was a birthday gift from my husband. He put it-- the picture to this lady. Later on, I asked, where do you have this little picture? He said, she lost it. So I don't have it anymore. In Canada, I think, I had the picture from somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is a picture of us by the middle one is-- her name is [? Lucia. ?] And the third one-- the third one is-- and the first one is Dora Nesselroth. And the third girl is now in [INAUDIBLE]. She has Alzheimer's Disease. Yeah. [SIGHS] It was in 1940, taking the picture. It was wintertime in 1940. We walked out of the ghetto to take a picture at a photographer studio, [BACKROUND NOISE] in Skarzysko-Kamienna. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father, taken in 1941, a ghetto ID card, photographer studio in Skarzysko-Kamienna. From the photographer negatives after the war, I have this drawing from a friend of ours. This was my idea. This was my-- all what I have. This was-- this was from high school girls who I have a speech there. And she sent me this, Love, and I put it here to my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this I-- because of him, when they took away my father, he was calling on me. He said, if you survive, don't forget to tell story, the real story what really happened here. And this is for him to remember. We cannot read this here, huh? Oh, to remember. And this is for him to remember. Oh god, help me. Now he would be about 90-something years old. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very early in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken after the war in 1950, Legonice, Poland, found after she died. I found the picture after she died. And this is the reason I took to a specialist who would make enlargement of the picture. And they-- a lot of things, they didn't want to do it. Because it's not allowed to make enlargement to a picture because the picture is too-- too young. I should tell it that the picture was taken in 1939. Maybe they would do it every-- everything. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is my mother. She was musically so inclined that she went to every-- she didn't read notes, you know. But she can play the piano, the only the violin she didn't play. But every music instrument, the guitar, everything she can play. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was a cook, ooh, the best one. And she was a mother, nothing to-- to brag about. She was so good. She was good for the neighbors. She was good for everybody. So was my grandmother. This is my family. From the left, a young girl, this is the Ruth's and Misha's daughter, here three of them. This is Misha on the left side, and my daughter Ruth. There you see, this is the other granddaughter. This is Rosalie's. Her name is Anna, Bart, Rosalie. Then Jackie is also their daughter. And this little one is Keren. And this is Shani. The last one here, you see me and my father-- in Atlanta three years ago. They came to visit me. I was very, very sick at that time. So look at me. I'm not sick-- I look like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they left, I was sick, sick, sick. The enthusiasm they came make me feel very good and healthy, and I-- I didn't tell them that I'm sick. I didn't tell them. But my son-in-law, Bart, said, Mother, what's wrong with you? Is something wrong with you? You look like you're dizzy. I said, no, I drink, like you drink. He was a little-- if he drink something. I said, I drink, so I'm dizzy. Yeah. So I have a beautiful family.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband who is my nurse, my lover, my husband, my boyfriend, everything in one, the best, I think. This is my daughter Ruth. She is in from Israel, Jerusalem. This is Bart and Rosalie. This is my older daughter. [SIGHS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Do you want to tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Federation of Montgomery, and Maxwell/Gunter [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Air--

speaker: interviewer
text: Air Force Base.

speaker: interviewee
text: Air Force Base [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] honored Dora Nesselroth-- oh god-- oh, here is very--

speaker: interviewer
text: We can read it. That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can read it? OK. So this was in 1995, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] in April. It was-- I was also at the governor's mansion too before. And then I went to their thing. I was in a wheelchair at that time. And it was fantastic, those-- those people, it was heaven to have Jews and non-Jews there at the-- at the synagogue. There was a synagogue there. And they were marvelous. I have also somebody, a doctor had to light a candle. They said, no. I don't want it. This belongs to Mrs. Nesselroth. And I have light a candle. And the newspapers there send me also the news about me. They make a little mistake. They talk-- like every newspaper does it. But the-- the rest of the-- it was a beautiful picture, a color picture, they sent me this thing. In about two weeks later I received this.
